policy_statement: Chile will close all coal-fired power generation plants nationwide no later than 2040, with closures continuing through 2024 and beyond.
mechanism_description: Chile commits to closing coal-fired power generation plants, reducing coal-based electricity generation and lowering power-sector emissions by displacing fossil generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy generation mix change (coal retirements) → ↓ coal combustion → ↓ CO₂ (and associated GHG) emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Coal-fired power plants are retired/closed, reducing or eliminating coal-based electricity generation and shifting supply to alternative sources (renewables, gas, imports, storage, demand response, etc.). (2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of coal lowers direct stack CO₂ emissions (and typically also reduces upstream fossil supply-chain emissions and co-emitted pollutants). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower grid emission intensity and absolute power-sector GHG emissions, contributing to reduced cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced power-sector GHG emissions from stopping coal combustion for electricity generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: coal plant closures reduce coal combustion, which directly reduces CO₂ emissions from electricity generation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while phaseouts can improve air quality or reduce fuel use (resource efficiency) and may have adaptation co-benefits (system resilience), the defining and largest climate effect is direct GHG mitigation via avoided fossil generation.  
3) Edge cases applied: if coal is replaced predominantly by another fossil source (e.g., unabated gas), mitigation benefits may be smaller but the mechanism remains emissions reduction from eliminating coal; if closures cause increased imports from coal-heavy grids, net benefits could be reduced—these are implementation/context issues, not a different primary mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: any policy that phases out coal power targets a reduction in coal-fired generation, whose principal climate impact is lower power-sector GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: details like replacement mix, reliability investments, worker transition, or co-pollutant outcomes require policy-specific evidence and are not used to change the primary category here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net emissions outcomes depend on what replaces coal (renewables/storage vs. unabated gas/coal-heavy imports) and on plant utilization prior to retirement; some policies frame closures as air-quality or health actions, but the dominant climate pathway remains mitigation via avoided coal combustion.
key_indicators: “closure of coal-fired power generation plants”; “will continue with 7 more by 2024”; “will conclude no later than 2040”; “closure of all coal plants nationwide”
co_benefits: Air quality and public health improvements from reduced co-pollutants (e.g., SO₂, NOₓ, PM)
instance_edge_case_notes: The excerpt reads as a commitment/phaseout schedule but does not state whether it is legally binding; classified as a regulatory mandate because it sets a definitive nationwide closure endpoint and schedule.
